content:
A step-by-step guide to setting up your environment, transforming data, and fixing common integration errors.

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Recently, I integrated Python inside Power BI and it completely transformed the way I analyze and visualize data.
3 Ways to Use Python in Power BI
Power BI allows Python integration in three different places, and each serves a different purpose:
Import or Load Data Using Python
Path: Home → Get Data → Search “Python” → Python Script → Connect
📌 Use this when you want to load a dataset using Python (e.g., from CSV, SQL, web scraping, API etc.) After connecting, paste your Python code → click OK → the data will be imported into Power BI.
Clean / Transform Data Using Python
Path: Home → Transform Data → (Power Query Window) → Transform tab → Run Python Script
📌 Use this when you want to clean or reshape data using Python functions. Example: remove duplicates, fill missing values, group operations, custom calculations etc.
Steps:

• Transform Data → opens Power Query Editor

• Go to Transform tab → click Run Python Script

• Paste Python code → click OK → result loads into Power Query
Visualize Data Using Python (Charts / ML) — First-Time Users
Power BI allows you to create Python-powered visuals inside your reports. For first-time users, follow these simple steps:
Steps:

• Locate the Python Visual → Report View → Build pane → click Python icon (Py)

• Enable Python Scripting → If using for the first time, Power BI will prompt you. Click OK

• Write & Run Your Script → Drag fields into Values, write Python code (Matplotlib / Seaborn), click Run → chart appears
Tip: Ensure your environment has the required libraries installed: pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, seaborn.
But I’ll be honest… it wasn’t smooth at first.
The first two options to integrate Python into Power BI (Get Data → Python Script, and Transform Data → Run Python Script) are fairly straightforward.
However, in the third option, creating Python visuals in the Build window, you might not see the Python icon initially.
Here’s how to fix it:

• Install Required Libraries ->Open your activated Power BI environment (PowerBI)->run-> pip install matplotlib seaborn

• Restart Power BI -> Close and reopen Power BI Desktop after installation.

• Check the Visualizations Pane-> After restart, the Python visual icon (Py) should now appear under the Visualizations pane.
Tip: Make sure your environment also has pandas and NumPy installed to avoid errors when running Python visuals.
Takeaway
Power BI + Python = Next-Level Insights Integrating Python into Power BI turns dashboards from static visuals into dynamic, data-driven insights. From data cleaning to advanced charts and even ML-powered visuals, the possibilities are endless!
If you are just starting out, don’t worry! Try building a simple Python chart first, and then gradually explore more advanced steps as you get comfortable.
